clients.name: Sunway Group

scenarioImpacts.base: Brent at $79.40/bbl inflates construction fuel and cement-kiln energy costs ~8–12% vs prior quarter. MYR at 3.9153 (weakening) increases USD-denominated material import costs. Property development pipeline at risk of margin compression; infrastructure segment partially insulated by fixed-price government contracts.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil spike toward $100/bbl raises Sunway's construction input cost base by est. 18–22%. Cement and steel surcharges compound margin pressure. MYR toward 4.20 vs USD would increase unhedged USD material exposure materially; revolving credit facility drawings likely to increase.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Brent at $150+ triggers cement and fuel surcharges across all project sites; est. MYR 280–350M incremental cost on in-flight portfolio. Property demand destruction from energy inflation; presales slowdown accelerates refinancing pressure on development-linked SPV bonds.

talkingPoints: Brent at $79.40/bbl is tracking +12% vs Q4 2025 average — Sunway's construction fuel and diesel procurement should be hedged for H2 2026; current unhedged exposure on active site count represents est. MYR 45–60M annualised risk per $10/bbl move.

MYR at 3.9153 and weakening (Iran shock risk premium) increases USD-denominated raw material imports — recommend accelerating foreign-currency procurement drawdowns from existing RCF before any further MYR softening.

Property development SPV bonds maturing 2027–2028 should be pre-financed now while iTraxx Asia IG at 132bp remains executable; Sunway's investment-grade positioning is a differentiator vs peers — window to term out SPV debt before spread widening extends further.

clients.name: IJM Corporation

scenarioImpacts.base: Plantation segment benefits modestly from higher CPO prices (palm oil tracking crude oil correlation); infrastructure segment faces higher diesel and bitumen costs. Shipping costs for palm oil exports rising with Regional Shipping index down 1.4% as re-routing costs emerge. MYR at 3.9153 creates mixed FX impact — exports benefit, USD imports hurt.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Brent at $100/bbl translates to CPO prices tracking $1,200+/MT — plantation revenue improves meaningfully but is offset by infrastructure cost inflation. Port and logistics operations face significantly higher bunker costs for vessel charters. Net: marginally negative at group level given infrastructure-heavy earnings mix.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Extended Hormuz closure redirects ASEAN-Middle East shipping; IJM's port concessions face volume decline. Plantation export logistics disrupted if Strait of Malacca re-routing adds transit costs. Refinancing of MYR 1.2bn infrastructure sukuk warrants urgent review if credit spreads blow out.

talkingPoints: CPO prices tracking crude oil upward (Brent at $79.40 → CPO correlation ~0.72); IJM's plantation segment is a natural hedge against oil shock — update earnings model to reflect revised CPO trajectory for H1 2026.

Infrastructure contract pipeline includes government-mandated projects with fixed-price clauses — recommend renegotiating fuel escalation provisions on active tenders before locking in sub-$90/bbl diesel assumptions in bid pricing.

MYR sukuk maturing 2026–2027: BOT-parallel pressure from BNM creates window to refinance at sub-4.5% before Iran shock recalibrates ASEAN EM rate expectations; recommend mandate selection within 30 days.

clients.name: Wilmar International

scenarioImpacts.base: Palm oil/biodiesel pricing directly tracks Brent at $79.40 — CPO refining margins improve as biodiesel mandates across Indonesia (B40) and Malaysia (B20) gain regulatory urgency. Shipping fuel (bunkers) represents ~12% of Wilmar's cost base; Regional Shipping index declining 1.4% signals re-routing cost pressure on Wilmar's 500+ vessel fleet operations.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil at $100/bbl creates dual impact: biodiesel margin expansion (+USD 85–100/MT) partially offset by bunker cost surge on owned/chartered fleet. Supply chain re-routing around Hormuz disruption adds 8–15 days to Middle East cargo legs. SGD at 1.2761 (strengthening) compresses USD revenues on translation.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Hormuz closure disrupts Wilmar's Middle East bulk trade routes; est. $200–280M incremental annual logistics cost at $150/bbl oil. Biodiesel revenue surge partially offsets — Indonesia emergency mandates could lift volumes 15–20%. Revolving credit facility utilisation spikes; bank appetite for commodity trader credit under sanctions pressure.

talkingPoints: Brent at $79.40/bbl makes Indonesia's B40 biodiesel mandate economically compelling — Wilmar is the largest biodiesel refiner in ASEAN; model revenue uplift from mandatory blending at current crude economics shows $320–380M incremental EBITDA vs $60/bbl base case.

Wilmar's fleet fuel exposure is significant: bunker cost is est. USD 1.1bn annually — a $15/bbl Brent move translates to ~$220M fuel cost delta; recommend reviewing existing hedging book and extending coverage to Q4 2026 given Hormuz disruption tail risk.

USD revolving credit facility: with iTraxx Asia IG at 132bp and widening, pre-emptive draw-down of undrawn RCF tranches locks in current margin; reduces refinancing risk if commodity trade finance bank appetite tightens under Iran secondary sanctions pressure.

clients.name: Hyflux

scenarioImpacts.base: Desalination operations are extremely energy-intensive — electricity costs represent 35–45% of total operating cost. Brent at $79.40/bbl drives Singapore grid power costs higher (+8–10% vs Q4 2025). Legacy restructuring means Hyflux balance sheet remains highly leveraged; restructuring creditor repayment schedule linked to operational cashflows now under pressure from energy cost inflation.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Power cost at $100/bbl oil equivalent could push desalination OPEX above contractual tariff recovery thresholds. Refinancing creditors become anxious; restructuring plan covenant compliance at risk. ASEAN HY spreads at 432bp make any new capital raise prohibitively expensive — further restructuring negotiation likely required.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Energy cost spiral under $150+ oil renders Hyflux's desalination economics non-viable without emergency tariff relief from government. Creditor acceleration risk materialises; Singapore government may need to intervene to ensure water supply continuity. Restructuring collapses into administration unless sovereign backstop committed.

talkingPoints: Hyflux desalination OPEX is 38–42% electricity; with Singapore power prices tracking Brent at $79.40, the cost-recovery gap vs contracted tariff has widened est. SGD 18–22M annually — urgent need to renegotiate power purchase agreements or escalation clauses in government supply contracts.

Restructuring plan repayment milestones fall due H2 2026 — ASEAN HY spreads at 432bp effectively close the capital markets window; explore alternative private credit or DFI/multilateral bridge financing to cover restructuring obligations before energy cost deterioration worsens creditor confidence.

Energy cost relief window: if Hormuz de-escalates within 4–6 weeks (Base scenario, 42% probability), Brent could retrace toward $68–72; restructuring advisor should model refinancing window contingent on sub-$75 oil trajectory to time capital market access optimally.

clients.name: Sapura Energy

scenarioImpacts.base: Brent at $79.40/bbl is above the $65–70 E&P capex trigger threshold for most upstream clients; new contract awards improving but execution lagging. Sapura's high leverage (net debt ~MYR 10bn) means rising rates and widening ASEAN HY spreads (432bp) directly impact refinancing cost. Shipping utilisation for marine vessels recovering modestly.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil at $100/bbl boosts E&P client confidence but capex cycle lag means Sapura revenue uplift is 6–9 months behind oil price. Existing debt refinancing under stress: ASEAN HY at 500bp+ would make any bond issuance economically unfeasible. MAS sanctions compliance review for any Iran-adjacent upstream client creates additional contract risk.
scenarioImpacts.tail: E&P capex freezes under $150 oil uncertainty (client hedging collapse); paradoxically negative for oil services despite high prices. Sapura debt covenant breach risk materialises; government-linked creditor (Petronas, Maybank) intervention required. Potential restructuring or asset sale of marine fleet to service debt obligations.

talkingPoints: Brent at $79.40/bbl is above Sapura's E&P client capex threshold — but order book recovery is lagging the oil price by 2–3 quarters; model 2026 revenue at $72/bbl equivalent to avoid overcounting near-term uplift in board presentations.

Sapura's MYR debt maturities 2026–2027 represent the critical refinancing risk: iTraxx Asia IG at 132bp and ASEAN HY at 432bp signals the window is still open for secured bank refinancing vs unsecured bond — recommend urgent bilateral bank discussions before market tightens further.

MAS issued emergency Iran sanctions guidance on Mar 3 — review all Sapura upstream contracts with Middle East or Iran-adjacent geography for secondary sanctions compliance; any contract with Iranian-linked JV partners requires immediate legal review to protect parent entity.

clients.name: PTTEP

scenarioImpacts.base: PTTEP is a net beneficiary of Brent at $79.40/bbl — upstream producer with production cost est. $28–32/bbl; operating margin expands materially vs prior-year $50/bbl price. THB at 31.58 (mild weakening) marginally benefits USD oil revenue on repatriation. LNG and Thailand domestic gas supply obligations provide stable domestic revenue floor.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil at $100/bbl boosts PTTEP upstream EBITDA by est. USD 400–550M annually. However, Iran proxy activation risks disrupting Gulf of Thailand LNG supply chain and creates sanctions compliance complexity for any Middle East JV operations. THB weakening toward 34 in stress reduces repatriation value.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Extended Hormuz closure raises PTTEP's shipping and logistics cost for LNG offtake; rerouting adds significant cost to Mozambique and Myanmar offshore logistics. BOT emergency intervention and THB depreciation could reduce USD revenue translation. Sanctions scrutiny on Middle East upstream JVs warrants immediate compliance review.

talkingPoints: PTTEP is directly benefiting from Brent at $79.40 — upstream production cost at $28–32/bbl means current oil price generates operating margins of $47–51/bbl; recommend accelerating hedging of 2026 production at current levels before any de-escalation retracement toward $65.

THB bond maturity H2 2026: BOT rate hold (inflation pressure from oil) means domestic refinancing at current 3.10% 10Y is the best available window — recommend pre-financing THB bond now before any Iran-driven inflation repricing forces BOT to hold rates higher for longer.

MAS guidance on Iran sanctions (Mar 3) may affect PTTEP's Gulf of Oman or Oman-adjacent upstream JV operations — conduct immediate sanctions jurisdictional review of all Middle East exploration assets and JV partner relationships to ensure full OFAC and EU compliance.
